slides.label: Title
slides.headline: Probability, Uncertainty, and the Confidence Illusion
slides.subtitle: Why your intuition forgets the prior, and what to do when it does.
slides.speaker_notes: TODO: open the lecture. Why your intuition forgets the prior, and what to do when it does.

slides.label: Learning objectives
slides.headline: Learning objectives
slides.subtitle: TODO: one line on what this part establishes.
slides.speaker_notes: Section divider.

slides.label: Learning objectives
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

- Apply Bayes' theorem to calculate the actual probability of a rare event given a positive test result, and explain why the result differs from naive intuition - Use the total probability theorem to decompose complex probability calculations into tractable pieces - Apply the multiplication (chain) rule to compute joint probabilities across sequential events - Identify the base rate in a deployment scenario and explain how ignoring it produces systematically wrong conclusions - Distinguish between model accuracy and model calibration, and describe what a calibration curve reveals that accuracy metrics hide - Recognize the conditions under which the Central Limit Theorem does not apply, and identify when a deployment's loss distribution is likely to be heavy-tailed - Explain distribution shift as an instance of Hume's problem of induction, and name at least two mechanisms by which a model can become confidently wrong after deployment
slides.speaker_notes: By the end of this chapter, you will be able to: - Apply Bayes' theorem to calculate the actual probability of a rare event given a positive test result, and explain why the result differs from naive intuition - Use the total probability theorem to decompose complex probability calculations into tractable pieces - Apply the multiplication (chain) rule to compute joint probabilities across sequential events - Identify the base rate in a deployment scenario and explain how ignoring it produces systematically wrong conclusions - Distinguish between model accuracy and model calibration, and describe what a calibration curve reveals that accuracy metrics hide - Recognize the conditions under which the Central Limit Theorem does not apply, and identify when a deployment's loss distribution is likely to be heavy-tailed - Explain distribution shift as an instance of Hume's problem of induction, and name at least two mechanisms by which a model can become confidently wrong after deployment

slides.label: Why this chapter
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: The failures we examined in Chapter 1 were not, for the most part, failures of model competence.
This chapter builds the apparatus for repairing those intuitions.
slides.speaker_notes: The failures we examined in Chapter 1 were not, for the most part, failures of model competence. The models were doing what their training permitted. The failures were failures of interpretation — of engineers and operators reading model outputs through intuitions that were not built for the problem at hand.

slides.label: The multiplication rule — building joint probabilities from conditionals
slides.headline: The multiplication rule — building joint probabilities from conditionals
slides.subtitle: TODO: one line on what this part establishes.

slides.label: The multiplication rule — building joint probabilities from
slides.eyebrow: The multiplication rule — building joint probabilities from conditionals
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: Rearranging the conditional probability definition gives us something immediately useful.
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$
then
slides.speaker_notes: Rearranging the conditional probability definition gives us something immediately useful. If $$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$ then $$P(A \cap B) = P(B) \cdot P(A \mid B)$$ This is the multiplication rule: to find the probability that both events occur, multiply the probability of the first by the conditional probability of the second given the first. It sounds simple.

slides.label: The multiplication rule — building joint probabilities from conditionals — equation
slides.headline: TODO: name the equation
slides.subhead: TODO: one line on what it is a statement about
slides.values_claim: TODO: the contestable value the equation encodes.

tangent.reentry: TODO: hand back to the main argument.

slides.speaker_notes: TODO: expand into an equation tangent (see EQUATIONS.md).
derivation_steps: P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap A_3) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2)
P\left(\bigcap_{i=1}^n A_i\right) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2 \mid A_1) \cdot P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdots P\left(A_n \mid \bigcap_{i=1}^{n-1} A_i\right)

slides.label: The total probability theorem — decomposing complex problems
slides.eyebrow: The total probability theorem — decomposing complex problems
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: Here is the next tool.

Let $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ be events that partition the sample space: they are mutually exclusive (no two can occur simultaneously), collectively exhaustive (one of them must occur), and each has positive probability.
$$P(B) = \sum_{i=1}^n P(A_i) \cdot P(B \mid A_i)$$
slides.speaker_notes: Here is the next tool. Sometimes you cannot compute $P(B)$ directly, but you can compute it by breaking the sample space into pieces. Let $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ be events that partition the sample space: they are mutually exclusive (no two can occur simultaneously), collectively exhaustive (one of them must occur), and each has positive probability.

It is a screening test for some disease — pick whichever one makes you uncomfortable.
Now: how confident are you that this patient has the disease?
slides.speaker_notes: Now I want to tell you about a test. It is a screening test for some disease — pick whichever one makes you uncomfortable. The disease is rare.

slides.label: Bayes' theorem — the bookkeeping made explicit
slides.headline: Bayes' theorem — the bookkeeping made explicit
slides.subtitle: TODO: one line on what this part establishes.

slides.label: Bayes' theorem — the bookkeeping made explicit
slides.eyebrow: Bayes' theorem — the bookkeeping made explicit
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: The calculation we just did has a name and a formula.
Let $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ partition the sample space, and let $B$ be any event with $P(B) > 0$.
$$P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(A_i) \cdot P(B \mid A_i)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(A_i) \cdot P(B \mid A_i)}{\sum_{k=1}^n P(A_k) \cdot P(B \mid A_k)}$$
slides.speaker_notes: The calculation we just did has a name and a formula. Let me write it down, because the formula makes explicit where the prior lives. Let $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$ partition the sample space, and let $B$ be any event with $P(B) > 0$.

slides.label: Bayes' theorem — the bookkeeping made explicit — equation
slides.headline: TODO: name the equation
slides.subhead: TODO: one line on what it is a statement about
slides.tex: P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(A_i) \cdot P(B \mid A_i)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(A_i) \cdot P(B \mid A_i)}{\sum_{k=1}^n P(A_k) \cdot P(B \mid A_k)}
slides.values_claim: TODO: the contestable value the equation encodes.

slides.caption: The positive test does real work: it raises the probability of disease a hundredfold. But the prior was so low that the posterior is still very small. Both facts are important — and neither is visible if you only read the test's accuracy number.

slides.label: Calibration — the most operationally important diagnostic in this chapter
slides.headline: Calibration — the most operationally important diagnostic in this chapter
slides.subtitle: TODO: one line on what this part establishes.
slides.speaker_notes: Section divider.

slides.label: Calibration — the most operationally important diagnostic in
slides.eyebrow: Calibration — the most operationally important diagnostic in this chapter
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: I want to spend time on a specific, operational idea — the most useful diagnostic in this chapter.
A model is calibrated when its stated probabilities match what actually happens.
You can see this in a picture.
slides.speaker_notes: I want to spend time on a specific, operational idea — the most useful diagnostic in this chapter. It is called calibration, and a model can be very accurate without being calibrated, and the difference matters. A model is calibrated when its stated probabilities match what actually happens.

slides.label: Figure — Calibration — the most operationally imp
slides.headline: TODO: what the figure shows
slides.caption: The well-calibrated region (left portion) is where the model's stated confidence is informative. The overconfident tail (right portion) is where it lies.
slides.alt: The well-calibrated region (left portion) is where the model's stated confidence is informative. The overconfident tail (right portion) is where it lies.
slides.speaker_notes: The well-calibrated region (left portion) is where the model's stated confidence is informative. The overconfident tail (right portion) is where it lies.

slides.label: When the Central Limit Theorem politely declines to help
slides.eyebrow: When the Central Limit Theorem politely declines to help
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: There is one more thing, because it is the place where standard-issue probability training will betray you.
If you have taken a stats class, you have met the Central Limit Theorem.
The theorem has two requirements buried in it.
slides.speaker_notes: There is one more thing, because it is the place where standard-issue probability training will betray you. If you have taken a stats class, you have met the Central Limit Theorem. It says, more or less, that if you add up a lot of independent random variables with finite variance and normalize them, the result looks like a Gaussian — the bell curve.

slides.headline: TODO: what the figure shows

slides.speaker_notes: In the heavy-tailed regime, the next observation can move your average dramatically no matter how many observations you have already collected.

slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: Most of the failures of confidence in deployed AI systems are not failures of model competence.
The technical content of this chapter — conditional probability, the chain rule, the total probability theorem, Bayes, calibration, heavy tails — is the apparatus for repairing those intuitions.
Four questions, in order:
slides.speaker_notes: Most of the failures of confidence in deployed AI systems are not failures of model competence. The models are doing what their architectures and training data permit them to do. The failures are failures of interpretation — of humans reading model outputs through intuitions not built for the problem at hand.

slides.label: What would change my mind
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: If a deployment-monitoring methodology emerged that reliably detected distribution shift in production from output statistics alone — without access to ground-truth outcomes — the calibration framing in this chapter would need revision.
I do not have a clean diagnostic for when a deployment's loss distribution is heavy-tailed before the catastrophic event reveals it.
slides.speaker_notes: If a deployment-monitoring methodology emerged that reliably detected distribution shift in production from output statistics alone — without access to ground-truth outcomes — the calibration framing in this chapter would need revision. Current methods detect a subset of shifts; the general problem is open. [Verify: Krell et al.

slides.label: Connections forward
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: A calibration curve tells you how confident the model should be.
That is Chapter 3.
slides.speaker_notes: A calibration curve tells you how confident the model should be. It does not tell you what the model is confident about — what it has noticed, what it has missed, whose face it works on and whose it does not. The model's outputs are shaped by its training data, and the training data was shaped by people who made choices, often without noticing they were making them.

slides.label: Chapter summary
slides.headline: TODO: a sharp claim, not the heading.
bullets: You can now do six things you could not do before this chapter.
You can apply conditional probability to compute the probability of one event given another, using the formal definition and the chain rule to handle sequential events.
You can calculate what a positive test result actually means, given a population base rate, and explain the false-positive structure that makes this relevant to every rare-event detection system.
slides.speaker_notes: You can now do six things you could not do before this chapter. You can apply conditional probability to compute the probability of one event given another, using the formal definition and the chain rule to handle sequential events. You can use the total probability theorem to decompose complex probabilities by conditioning on a partition of the sample space.
